title: 10 best herbal essential oils for bitter taste in mouth
intro_desc: Herbal essential oils for bitter taste in mouth are natural remedies derived from plants that help alleviate the unpleasant sensation of bitterness in the mouth. These essential oils contain bioactive compounds that reduce inflammation, soothe the mucous membranes, and balance the taste buds. The best herbal essential oils for bitter taste in mouth include Zingiber officinale, Mentha x piperita, Cinnamomum verum, Curcuma longa, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Piper nigrum, Syzygium aromaticum, Cinnamomum zeylanicum, Elettaria cardamomum, and Cuminum cyminum. If left untreated, bitter taste in mouth can lead to social withdrawal, difficulty in eating, and decreased quality of life, significantly impacting one's overall well-being and daily interactions.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Myrrh essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. When the tongue or mouth is infected by bacteria, it can cause an unpleasant bitter taste. The antimicrobial properties of myrrh oil help to eliminate these bacteria, reducing the bitterness and promoting a healthy mouth environment. Additionally, myrrh oil's anti-inflammatory properties soothe and calm the mucous membranes in the mouth, further alleviating any discomfort or irritation associated with a bitter taste.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Myrrh essential oil has antimicrobial properties that help combat bacteria, viruses, and fungi that can cause bad breath and bitter taste by reducing their growth and eliminating them from the oral cavity.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Myrrh essential oil's anti-inflammatory properties help reduce swelling and pain in the gums, tongue, and other oral tissues, which can be caused by infection, injury, or gum disease, and contribute to a bitter taste.
Antioxidant properties: Myrrh essential oil's antioxidant properties help protect the mouth and oral cavity from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to a bitter taste and dry mouth.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in sesquiterpenes, which provide a strong, bitter flavor and aroma ideal for oral health.
Buds: Containing high concentrations of resinous compounds, which help to reduce inflammation and freshen the breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh commiphora myrrha resin from the tree and dry it properly for optimal oil extraction.
Grind 100 grams of dried commiphora myrrha resin into fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Combine the powdered commiphora myrrha resin with 500 milliliters of carrier oil such as sweet almond oil in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture for 14 days at room temperature, shaking the jar daily to facilitate oil extraction from the resin.
Strain and decant the resulting oil through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle, discarding the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial properties that help to eliminate any lingering bacteria or toxins that may be causing the bitterness. Additionally, thyme oil's decongestant and anti-inflammatory qualities can reduce inflammation in the tongue and mouth, further alleviating bitter tastes. The oil's soothing properties also help to calm any irritation or discomfort caused by bitter substances, promoting a fresher and more pleasant oral environment.
remedy_properties: Antibacterial properties: Thyme essential oil helps to combat bacteria that can cause bad breath and bitter taste by inhibiting their growth, reducing the severity of oral infections.

Antioxidant properties: Thyme essential oil's antioxidant properties help to neutralize free radicals that can contribute to oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, which can lead to a bitter taste sensation.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Thyme essential oil's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce swelling and discomfort associated with oral infections, gum disease, or other conditions that may cause bitter taste, promoting overall oral health.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the thyme plant are rich in sesquiterpenes, which contribute to its bitterness and earthy flavor, making them suitable for creating essential oils that alleviate bitter taste.

Buds: Thyme buds contain a high concentration of flavonoids and phenolic acids, which are responsible for their astringent and bitter properties, making them ideal for creating medicinal essential oils.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh thymus vulgaris leaves and flowers carefully avoiding contamination and excessive moisture.
Steam distill the harvested plant material using a clevenger apparatus for approximately 4-6 hours.
Collect the resulting essential oil in a clean glass container with a tight-fitting lid and label accurately.
Filter the essential oil through a coffee filter or paper towel to remove any impurities or debris.
Store the filtered essential oil in a dark glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid and keep it away from direct sunlight.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because its potent menthol properties stimulate saliva production, washing away lingering bitter flavors. Additionally, peppermint's natural antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties soothe the tongue and mouth tissues, reducing inflammation that can contribute to bitterness. Dry the harvested leaves in a warm oven or with air circulation for 24-48 hours to reduce moisture.
Crush the dried leaves into small pieces using a mortar and pestle or food processor to increase surface area.
Steam distill the crushed leaves in a still apparatus with pure water at a ratio of 1:5 for 2-3 hours.

Chop the dried plant material into small pieces and transfer it to a glass jar with a lid containing 10 grams of chopped plant matter per 100 milliliters of carrier oil.

Discard the solids and store the resulting essential oil in a dark glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid, ready for use as a natural remedy for bitter taste relief.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried artemisia absinthium leaves and stems measuring one part of the herb to two parts of carrier oil.

Allow the mixture to steep in a cool dark place for 14 days, shaking the jar daily to promote infusion, before straining the liquid through a cheesecloth into another container.

Distill the filtered liquid using a low-pressure steam distillation apparatus for 3 hours to produce a total of 10 milliliters of echinacea angustifolia essential oil.

Cinnamaldehyde: As a terpene with analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, it helps to numb the pain and discomfort associated with bitter taste in the mouth.
Linalool: This terpene acts as a natural anxiolytic and has anti-inflammatory properties, helping to calm the nervous system and reduce the perception of bitter taste in the mouth.
remedy_parts: Buds: Buds are used to make essential oils due to their high concentration of eugenol, a compound that has anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties.
Flowers: Flowers are used to make essential oils because they contain a high amount of eugenol and other compounds that help to reduce inflammation and alleviate mouth sores.

Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes are used to make essential oils due to their high content of eugenol and other compounds that have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce the bitterness in the mouth.
remedy_recipe: Harvest syzygium aromaticum flowers in large quantities at dawn when dew is still present on the petals.
Dry the flowers by spreading them out in a single layer on a paper tray for 7 days.
Steam distill 100g of dried flowers in 1 liter of distilled water at 100c for 3 hours.
Collect the resulting essential oil and separate it from water using a separating funnel.
Filter the essential oil through a coffee filter paper to remove any remaining impurities and particles.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinnamomum zeylanicum essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The oil contains compounds like cinnamaldehyde, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and kill bacteria that can cause bad breath and bitter taste. Additionally, the oil's decongestant and expectorant properties can help alleviate respiratory issues that may contribute to a bitter taste. This makes Cinnamomum zeylanicum essential oil a popular natural remedy for bad breath and mouth health.
remedy_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: This key constituent helps with bitter taste in mouth by exhibiting antimicrobial properties that can reduce the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath and mouth odor.
Eugenol: Eugenol helps with bitter taste in mouth by its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce pain and inflammation in the mouth, leading to a more pleasant taste experience.

Cinnamyl acetate: Cinnamyl acetate contributes to the reduction of bitter taste in mouth by exhibiting a sweet and spicy flavor, which can mask unpleasant tastes and provide a more refreshing experience.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: They are used to make essential oils due to their high concentration of cinnamaldehyde, a compound responsible for the bitter and warm taste.
Barks: The bark is used to produce essential oils, which contain compounds like cinnamaldehyde, contributing to the bitter taste.
Leaves: The leaves are another part of Cinnamomum zeylanicum used to make essential oils, containing compounds that add to the bitter taste.

remedies.remedy_desc: Elettaria cardamomum essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural deodorizing and anti-inflammatory properties. It contains compounds like limonene and linalool that have been found to neutralize volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) responsible for the bitter taste. The oils also exhibit antibacterial properties, which can help reduce the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath and a bitter mouth. By reducing the levels of VSCs and bacteria, Elettaria cardamomum essential oils can leave the mouth feeling fresher and cleaner.
remedy_constituents: Limonene: Helps to reduce the perception of bitter taste by suppressing the bitter taste receptors on the tongue.
Borneol: Acts as a bitter taste inhibitor by blocking the bitter taste sensation and reducing the unpleasant taste experience.
Terpinen-4-ol: Exhibits anti-bitter properties by inhibiting the bitter taste receptors and providing a fresh, minty flavor that counteracts bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes contain essential oils like limonene, which is responsible for the bitter taste in mouth.
Seeds: Seeds of Elettaria cardamomum contain essential oils like limonene and linalool, contributing to the bitter taste.
Buds: Buds of the plant, especially the young shoots, are used to make essential oils, which contain compounds like limonene and beta-pinene, contributing to the bitter taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 100g of fresh elettaria cardamomum pods when they are mature and fragrant.
Dry the harvested pods in a warm and well-ventilated area for 7 to 10 days.
Steam distill the dried elettaria cardamomum pods at 100-110°c for 3 to 4 hours.
Collect the distilled essential oil and separate it from the water using a separator.
Filter the essential oil through a paper filter to remove any impurities and sediment.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cuminum cyminum essential oils helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The oil's active compounds, such as limonene and beta-pinene, have been shown to stimulate saliva production, neutralizing acids and reducing bitterness. Additionally, the oil's antimicrobial properties help to combat the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms that can contribute to a bitter taste. As a result, Cuminum cyminum essential oils can provide relief from bitter taste, promoting a healthy and fresh mouth.
remedy_constituents: Cuminum: It helps to reduce bitterness in mouth by its carminative properties, which aid in digestion and relieve flatulence, thus minimizing the perception of bitterness.
Limonene: It helps to reduce bitterness in mouth by its ability to act as a volatile oil that can stimulate digestion and relieve indigestion, thereby reducing the intensity of bitter taste.

Caryophyllene oxide: It helps to reduce bitterness in mouth by its anti-inflammatory properties, which can soothe irritated mucous membranes and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, thus reducing the sensation of bitterness.
remedy_parts: Seeds: The seeds are commonly used to make essential oils due to their rich content of volatile compounds, including limonene and beta-pinene, which contribute to the bitter taste.
Leaves: Cumin leaves are often used to create essential oils with a bitter and earthy flavor profile, thanks to their rich concentration of sesquiterpenes and other volatile compounds.
Stems: The stems of the cumin plant contain essential oils that contribute to the bitter taste, particularly due to the presence of sesquiterpenes and other volatile organic compounds.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 50g of cuminum cyminum seeds at the peak of maturity when the seeds are fully ripe and dry.
Dry the harvested seeds in a warm place with good air circulation for 2-3 days.
Grind the dried seeds into a fine powder using a high-speed grinder.
Steam distill the ground seeds at 100-150°c for 2-3 hours using a steam distillation apparatus.
Collect and filter the essential oil from the distillate using a separatory funnel and filter paper.
